column1: What does the closed-loop heater lab add to the thermometer?
column2: An actuator: the dispatcher fans the measured temperature out to two consumers — the display and a new heater task — and the heater task switches a resistive heating element on or off to hold a set-point.

column1: What is fan-out in the heater lab?
column2: A single producer pushing the same reading to two consumer queues — one Get from the acquisition queue, then two Puts (display queue and control queue) — so both the display and the heater task receive the temperature.

column1: How is the heating element driven?
column2: Through a load switch — the kind of low-side or high-side transistor stage covered in the load-switches chapter.

column1: What is bang-bang control with hysteresis?
column2: The simplest closed loop: turn the heater on below (setpoint − h) and off above (setpoint + h). The dead-band h stops the output chattering when the temperature sits at the set-point.

column1: Why is hysteresis the whole trick in bang-bang control?
column2: With a single threshold the heater would switch thousands of times a second as noise crosses the line. The ±h band trades a little temperature ripple for a sane switching rate.

column1: Is bang-bang the best controller, and what is next?
column2: It is the floor, not the ceiling. When overshoot or ripple matters you move to proportional or PID control — but the task/queue plumbing built here is exactly what a PID loop would reuse.

column1: How does the lab make the heater state visible?
column2: An LED (led0) mirrors the heater on/off state so you can see the control loop working.
